does: This paper presents the first empirical analysis of prompt injection attacks against agentic AI coding editors like Cursor that operate with elevated system privileges. The authors introduce AIShellJack, an automated testing framework containing 314 unique attacks designed to assess vulnerabilities in these editors. The core contribution demonstrates how attackers can remotely exploit agentic AI systems by poisoning external development resources with malicious instructions, causing the AI agents to execute arbitrary commands on the developer's system. The paper empirically characterizes the attack surface, showing how seemingly benign external resources such as README files, configuration files, or dependency documentation can be weaponized to hijack AI agents and execute attacker-controlled shell commands.
connects: This work builds directly on recent research examining prompt injection vulnerabilities in language models, extending prior findings from chat interfaces and general LLM applications to the specialized domain of agentic coding editors. It responds to growing concerns about LLM security as these systems gain expanded capabilities and system access beyond text generation. The paper connects to research on AI agent safety and privilege escalation, as well as prior work on code injection attacks and supply chain security in software development. It also relates to emerging literature on the risks of deploying language models in trusted environments where they can interact with external systems and execute potentially dangerous operations.
enables: This research enables future work on defense mechanisms against prompt injection attacks in agentic systems, including input sanitization, prompt hardening, and privilege restriction strategies. It points toward the need for security-aware design principles in agentic AI editors, potentially motivating sandboxing approaches and dynamic privilege verification before executing sensitive commands. The findings could drive development of detection systems to identify when agents have been compromised by malicious prompts. More broadly, this work establishes a foundation for security testing frameworks applicable to other high-privilege agentic AI systems beyond code editors, and highlights the critical need to balance developer productivity gains against security risks when deploying autonomous AI agents with system access.
